items.q: What is an adjacency pair in conversation analysis?
items.correct: A two-part sequence where the first part conditions the second — question/answer, greeting/greeting, request/acceptance-or-refusal. The second part is 'conditionally relevant' on the first.
items.misconception: Adjacency pairs are two people who sit next to each other while talking.
items.random_wrong: Adjacency pairs are pairs of synonyms in vocabulary lists.
items.why: Adjacency pairs reveal that turns aren't independent — a question expects an answer, and silence after one is heard as meaningful. Students take 'adjacency' too literally (physical proximity) rather than structural sequencing.

items.q: What did Chomsky propose about language acquisition?
items.correct: Children possess an innate Language Acquisition Device — an inborn capacity for grammar — because exposure alone cannot account for how rapidly and uniformly children learn complex syntax.
items.misconception: Chomsky said children learn language entirely by copying adults.
items.random_wrong: Chomsky said children should be taught grammar from age two.
items.why: Chomsky's theory is precisely the opposite of behaviourist imitation. Students confuse Chomsky with Skinner — Chomsky's argument is that imitation cannot explain acquisition.

items.q: What is a speech act according to Searle/Austin?
items.correct: An utterance that performs an action — promising, ordering, apologising, declaring. Speech acts have locutionary (what's said), illocutionary (intended force) and perlocutionary (effect) dimensions.
items.misconception: A speech act is any time someone speaks.
items.random_wrong: A speech act is a performance or a play.
items.why: Speech act theory analyses how language DOES things, not just describes things. 'I now pronounce you married' is a declaration, not a description. Students miss that utterances can be acts in themselves.

items.q: A question not needing an answer is.
items.why: Correct answer: Rhetorical. “Tag” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Three items in a row is called a.
items.why: Correct answer: Triplet. “Couplet” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many techniques listed (RQ, direct address, repetition, triplet, hyperbole, statistic)?
items.why: Exact answer: 6. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
